senses.definition: to feel upset or regretful about something that has already happened and cannot be undone

senses.definition: to do good things and waste valuable resources for ungrateful people
(of a business) to reach a point that yields no success due to the profit being almost as equal as the costs

senses.definition: to try hard yet achieve very little or nothing at all

examples.x: I've been trying to finish this project for hours, but I feel like I'm just chasing my tail.

senses.definition: to avoid wasting one's time and energy by not trying to persuade or convince someone who is unlikely to change their opinion or behavior

examples.x: I knew that, so I saved my breath, and offered no explanations.
Save your breath, Tom.

word_list.word: [close|shut|lock] the (stable|barn) door after the horse (has bolted|is stolen)

senses.definition: to try to prevent something bad from happening after it has already happened
the point or situation from which one starts to do something

examples.x: After the project failed, they had to go back to square one and rethink their strategy.
It feels like I'm at square one again after losing all my data.
The team realized their plan wasn‚Äôt working and returned to square one.
Starting from square one, she began to build her career from scratch.
Every time we encounter a major setback, we find ourselves back at square one.

senses.definition: used when one has to return to the beginning of a process and redo the process after one's effort has failed

word_list.pronunciation: f…Ļ Ćm pňą…™l…ö t…ô pňąo äst

senses.definition: from one place or situation to another without a clear purpose or direction, often in a chaotic or haphazard manner

examples.x: After the storm, the insurance company sent us from pillar to post trying to get our claim approved.
The job seeker has been going from pillar to post, attending job fairs and submitting resumes, but has yet to receive any job offers.
The refugee family has been moving from pillar to post, seeking asylum in different countries, but has been turned away at every border.

senses.definition: to unnecessarily adorn or decorate something that is already beautiful or perfect

examples.x: The design of the dress was already elegant, but adding more sequins and beading would just paint the lily.
The food at the restaurant was already delicious, but adding more spices and flavors would gild the lily and ruin the balance of the dish.
The garden was already beautiful, but adding more flowers and decorations would just paint the lily and make it look cluttered.
The speech was already moving, but adding more dramatic language and gestures would gild the lily and make it seem insincere.

word_list.pronunciation: fňąe…™s …Ēňź…Ļ kňą Ćm t ä …Ēňź…Ļ hňą…™t …ź …Ēňź…Ļ √į…ô b…Ļňą…™k …Ēňź…Ļ wňą…Ēňźl

senses.definition: to reach a point where it is unlikely or too difficult to make any progress

examples.x: Despite their best efforts, the researchers hit a brick wall in trying to find a cure for the disease.
The team's efforts to implement a new strategy hit a brick wall due to resistance from key stakeholders.
The company's attempts to enter the competitive market faced a brick wall due to strong competition and market saturation.
The aspiring author faced a brick wall in getting their manuscript published, receiving rejection after rejection from publishers.
The company's expansion plans hit a wall when economic downturns affected the availability of investment capital.
After weeks of rapid progress, the research team suddenly hit a wall when they encountered unexpected technical challenges.

senses.definition: to take part in a protest that has no chance of reaching success

examples.x: The team's efforts to win the championship were like barking at the moon, as they were vastly outmatched by their opponents.
Trying to convince her to change her mind is like barking at the moon - it's a waste of time and effort.

senses.definition: to make a wrong decision about how to achieve or deal with something

examples.x: If you think I can help you with that, you're barking up the wrong tree - I have no expertise in that area.
The police were barking up the wrong tree when they initially suspected the wrong person of committing the crime.
You're barking up the wrong tree by blaming her for the mistake.
If you think I'm going to help you cheat, you're barking up the wrong tree.
The critics are barking up the wrong tree by questioning the artist's talent.

senses.definition: to waste one's time and energy for something that has no chance of achieving success or yeilds no profit

examples.x: The politician kept bringing up a scandal that had already been resolved, and was accused of beating a dead horse to score political points.
The team had already decided to change their strategy, but the coach kept talking about the old approach - it was like beating a dead horse.

senses.definition: to do something that is considered to be redundant or useless

examples.x: Buying more drinks for the party is like bringing sand to the beach - there's already plenty to go around.
The company's attempt to create a new product that was already available from many competitors was like bringing sand to the beach.

word_list.pronunciation: kňą Ćm …°ňĆo ä tňą…úňźn fňą äl sňą…úňźk…ôl

senses.definition: to go through a series of efforts or actions only to end up back where one started, highlighting the lack of progress or meaningful change

examples.x: After years of traveling and living abroad, she came full circle and returned to her hometown to start a family.
The artist's work had gone full circle, as they had returned to the style and themes that they had explored early in their career.
The city had turned full circle, as it had undergone a revitalization that had brought it back to its former glory as a cultural center.
The company's focus on customer service had come full circle, as they returned to the original values that had made them successful in the first place.
The political party had gone full circle, as it had returned to the original principles and values that had guided it in its early days.

senses.definition: the act of repeatedly talking or thinking about something without reaching a clear conclusion

examples.x: The boss was toing and froing between two candidates for the job, unable to decide which one was the better fit.
The couple was toing and froing about where to go on vacation, unable to make a decision on a destination.
The student was toing and froing between different majors, unsure of what career path to take.

senses.definition: used to refer to a task that is s very difficult or impossible to do

examples.x: The project was so disorganized and chaotic that it was like nailing Jell-O to the wall to get anything done.

word_list.pronunciation: lňąa…™k …Ēňź…Ļ pňąoňź…Ļ…™Ňč wňą…Ēňź…ĺ…ö…Ļ …™n …Ēňź…Ļ ňĆ…™nt ä …ź sňą…™v

senses.definition: used when someone is doing something that is pointless or ineffective

examples.x: Danny's mother used to say that teaching him good behaviour was like pouring water into a sieve.

word_list.pronunciation: lňąa…™k tňą…Ēňźk…™Ňč t ä …ź b…Ļňą…™k wňą…Ēňźl

senses.definition: used to describe a frustrating or unproductive conversation with someone who is unresponsive or uninterested

examples.x: The teacher's attempts to engage the disinterested student were like talking to a brick wall - the student was unresponsive and disengaged.
Trying to reason with him was like talking to a wall - he refused to listen to any other perspective.

senses.definition: the outcome or reward of a task is not worth the effort or resources required to achieve it
to fail to make any progress in a job or task
